elements.sent-2411-002: For each property that an independence relation can have we give assumptions on the functor that guarantee the property to be lifted.
elements.sent-2411-003: Frequently in mathematics the notion of independence comes up.
elements.sent-2411-004: For example: linear independence, algebraic independence, or probabilistic independence.
elements.sent-2411-005: In model theory, specifically in stability theory, independence plays a central role.
elements.sent-2411-006: Here it takes the form of the abstract notion of forking as developed by Shelah \cite{Sh , and the aforementioned examples are specific instances of this general theory.}
elements.sent-2411-007: Initially, forking was developed for the very well-behaved class of stable theories.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the late nineties Kim and Pillay generalised this work to the class of simple theories, a class that strictly contains the class of stable theories \cite{KP .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Much more recently, Kaplan and Ramsey defined a notion of Kim-forking, allowing for a generalisation to the class of NSOP$_1$ theories \cite{KapRam , which strictly contains the simple theories.}
elements.sent-2411-010: While the notions of forking and Kim-forking are heavily syntactic, the independence relation that they yield allows for purely semantic treatment.
elements.sent-2411-011: This goes back to Harnik and Harrington's work \cite{HH on independence in stable theories, where it is proved that a theory is stable if and only if an independence relation satisfying a certain list of properties exists and that independence relation is unique.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Later, si\-mi\-lar fully semantic treatments of independence was given in simple theories, by Kim and Pillay \cite{KP , and in NSOP$_1$ theories, by Dobrowolski and the first named author \cite{DK .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Again, we get that a theory is simple (resp.\ NSOP$_1$) if and only if there is an independence relation satisfying one less property (resp.\ two less properties) compared to the stable case, and this independence relation is unique.
elements.sent-2411-014: Such a unique independence relation is called the canonical independence relation.
elements.sent-2411-015: Nowadays, we use Makkai's notation \cite{M for independence relations, namely the $\ind$ symbol.}
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, we can define the independence relation $\ind^{\lin}$ given by linear independence in vector spaces as follows.
elements.sent-2411-017: If $A, B, C$ are subsets of a vector space $V$, then we write \begin{equation}A \ind^{\lin, V}_C B \quad \Longleftrightarrow \linspan(A \cup C) \cap \linspan(B \cup C) = \linspan(C).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is indeed the canonical independence relation for vector spaces.
elements.sent-2411-019: That is, the unique independence relation witnessing that the theory is stable.
elements.sent-2411-020: Generally, $A \ind^V_C B$ should be read as ``$A$ is independent from $B$ over $C$ in $V$'', and can intuitively be understood as ``all the information that $B$ has about $A$ is already contained in $C$''.
elements.sent-2411-021: The semantic treatment of independence relations opened new ways of studying them.
elements.sent-2411-022: In particular, a category-theoretic treatment became possible.
elements.sent-2411-023: As the original motivation for accessible categories was to develop a ``categorical model theory'' \cite{MP , it should be no surprise that it is the framework of accessible categories in which the categorical treatment of independence relations takes place.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In the combined work of \cite{LRV, K, K1 , canonicity of categorical independence is proved, which roughly states that in an accessible category there can be only one independence relation with the properties that independence is known to have in NSOP$_1$, simple or stable theories (see also Fact \ref{canonicity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: These canonicity results tell us what the correct definitions for categorical independence are, allowing for a new categorical approach to independence.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, in \cite{LRV1 a categorical construction of stable independence is given, and in \cite{KR the same is done for (potentially unstable) simple and NSOP$_1$-like independence.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In this context, it should also be mentioned that substantial work on independence has been done in the context of AECs (e.g., \cite{ShAEC, V, HytKes, BGKV ), which is a different framework from the categorical one, but allows for a direct translation \cite{BR .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this paper we contribute to the work on categorical independence by providing various criteria along which properties of an independence relation lift along a functor.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is best illustrated with an example (which is essentially the well-known model-theoretic example of the random graph).
elements.sent-2411-030: Let $F: \Gra_{\Emb \to \Set_{\Mono}$ be the forgetful functor from graphs with embeddings to sets with monomorphisms.}
elements.sent-2411-031: There is a stable independence relation $\ind$ on $\Set_{\Mono}$, which yields an independence relation $F^{-1 (\ind)$ on $\Gra_{\Emb}$ by declaring something independent if its image under $F$ is $\ind$-independent.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The question then becomes: what properties of $\ind$ lift to $F^{-1 (\ind)$? In this case the answer will be: all the properties of a stable independence relation, except for one (uniqueness).. Main results.}
elements.sent-2411-033: For each property that an independence relation can have we give assumptions on the functor $F$ that guarantee the property to be lifted.
elements.sent-2411-034: For a good number of properties (uniqueness, existence and 3-amalgamation) there are two flavours of assumptions: we can either assume $F$ to be a left multiadjoint, or we can assume something about the image of $F$, like a higher dimensional variant of cofinality.
